source.title: Attentional modulation of neuronal variability in circuit models of cortex

confidence_rationale: Kanashiro et al. show that top-down attentional signals modulate neural gain through inhibitory circuits, affecting both response magnitude and variability. This supports MECH-152's multiplicative gain control principle (terrain_weight scales E3 scoring channels). However, the paper addresses visual attention, not harm/goal terrain weighting.
failure_signatures: The paper models attention as gain control on sensory responses, not contextual modulation of harm/goal evaluation channels.
Gain control in the model operates through inhibitory circuits (SOM/PV interneurons), while MECH-152 uses a simple trained linear projection -- the computational mechanisms are very different.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Top-down attentional signals modulate neural gain through inhibitory circuits, primarily affecting inhibitory neurons. This produces both increased stimulus response gain and decreased noise correlations -- diverse correlates of attention arise from a single biophysical mechanism.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-152's terrain_weight signal [w_harm, w_goal] operates as a multiplicative gain control on E3's harm and goal scoring channels. The gain control principle described here -- top-down modulation scaling the response of downstream processing channels -- is the general computational mechanism MECH-152 implements in a specialized context (harm/goal terrain evaluation).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper addresses visual attentional gain control, not value/terrain-based modulation. The inhibitory circuit mechanism (SOM/PV interneuron interactions) has no direct analogue in MECH-152's linear projection architecture. The mapping is at the functional level (gain control principle), not the mechanistic level.
mapping.source_context: Computational neuroscience model of cortical circuits with excitatory and inhibitory populations, validated against V4 attention data

confidence_components.notes: eLife paper with solid computational methodology. Mapping fidelity low-moderate because the principle (gain control) is relevant but the specific domain (visual attention) and mechanism (inhibitory circuits) are distant from MECH-152. Transfer risk moderate.
